1: Bildad’s third and shortest speech (6 verses) reflects the exhaustion of the friends’ theological resources. Despite its brevity, it asks the most important question in the book: how can a human being be righteous before God? In the NT this question is not dismissed but fully answered in Christ: Romans 3:21–26 (the righteousness of God through faith in Christ); 2 Corinthians 5:21 (“he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God”).
2: God’s dominion and awesome power making peace in the heights — the sovereign peace of the divine order. Colossians 1:19–20: “in him all the fullness of God was pleased to dwell, and through him to reconcile to himself all things, whether on earth or in heaven, making peace by the blood of his cross.” The divine peace in the heights that Bildad describes abstractly is the peace that Christ accomplished concretely — the reconciliation of all things through the cross, including the heavenly realm.
3: The innumerable armies of God — the unlimited divine power. Hebrews 1:6: “let all God’s angels worship him” (Christ, at his entrance into the world). Matthew 26:53: Jesus at his arrest: “do you think that I cannot appeal to my Father, and he will at once send me more than twelve legions of angels?” The unlimited divine armies that Bildad invokes as evidence of human smallness are the armies that stood at Christ’s disposal and which he refused to summon in order to accomplish redemption by a different means.
4: “How then can man be righteous before God? How can he who is born of woman be pure?” — the question the Incarnation answers. Romans 3:21–22: “the righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ for all who believe.” Galatians 4:4: “born of woman, born under the law, to redeem those under the law, so that we might receive adoption as sons.” The “born of woman” who is pure is Christ; and those united to him receive his purity credited to them.
5: Even the moon is not bright and the stars are not pure in God’s eyes — the cosmic purity hierarchy. Revelation 21:23: “the city has no need of sun or moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light, and its lamp is the Lamb.” The moon and stars whose light falls short of divine purity will be superseded by the light of Christ himself as the lamp of the new creation. The hierarchy of brightness reaches its summit not in the stars but in the Lamb whose glory outshines them all.
6: “How much less man, who is a maggot, and the son of man, who is a worm!” — the nadir of human worth before divine holiness. Psalms 22:6: “I am a worm and not a man” — the suffering righteous one’s self-description in the paradigmatic passion psalm. Colossians 1:15: “he is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation.” The worm-son-of-man is the one whom Psalms 22:6 anticipates and who is identified with the divine image and firstborn of creation. The maximum creaturely humility (“worm”) and the maximum creaturely dignity (“image of God”) meet in Christ.
